posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked: Planning this as a single blind tasting for one of my wine groups. The question is what would be a reasonable vintage to be fair to show the wines at their best?

posts.name: Anton Dotson
posts.cooked:
I have no answer but wanted to say what a great idea!

Kudos!

posts.name: Craig Gleason
posts.cooked: Probably not the right vintage in general, but 89 Bahans would be a great wine to have in this tasting, or at any other time!

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: 2000 isn’t bad if prices are reasonable. If too pricey then 2001.

posts.name: Russell Faulkner
posts.cooked:
We did something similar in London years ago.

We didn’t manage to get them all from one vintage but ‘paired’ them up.

posts.name: Vince T s 3 n g
posts.cooked: Cool idea. When you say lesser growths, are you talking value buys in the 4th/5th or super seconds?

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
I am in the preliminary stages, but thinking of doing 3 pairs within a flight of six, so we would score the pairs and then the six overall.


First Flight
Petit Mouton against Lynch Bages (poor man’s Mouton)
Forts de Latour and GPL
Lafite and either Ducru (similar style) or Pontet Canet (contiguous)

Not sure about the second flight.One thought is to include Palmer as the Alter Ego thread was what started me thinking about the tasting.

Margaux, Pavilion and Issan
Palmer, Alter Ego and Rauzan Segla.

Alternatively
Alter Ego and Rauzan Segla
Pavilion Rouge and Rauzan Segla
Bahans Haut Brion and Haut Bailly


Alternatively
Margaux, Pavilion and Palmer
Haut Brion, Bahans, Haut Bailly.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: 2001 is drinking very well across a wide range of wines.

posts.name: Tim McCracken
posts.cooked: I would suggest a year no older than 1996 and no younger than 2004. If you want to go with the bigger wines, then 96 (left bank only) or 00. If you want to go for the more classic style, then 99, 01, 02 or 04. The heat of 2003 makes the year too variable, so I would avoid that as a benchmark.

posts.name: J Wei
posts.cooked:
I agree with 1996. If not 1996, then 2000.

No Leoville Barton or Poyferre in your tasting? I think Malescot is a great Margaux comparison as well. And for Graves, Smith Haut Lafitte would be good.

posts.name: Marcus Dean
posts.cooked: 1996 sounds about right, a lot of the second wines were pretty average in 89 and 90

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
How about Forts de Latour vs. LLC, which is right next door.

For vintages, how about 1990 or 2001.

posts.name: Marcu$ Stanley
posts.cooked: I would pair the second wines up against cheaper lesser growths – e.g. think it’s better to pair F d Latour up against GPL, which is significantly less expensive, then against a super second like LLC which can easily exceed F d Latour price in top vintages. Don’t think it would surprise anyone that something like LLC is better than a second wine of a first growth. But I suspect the value proposition for lower growths is a whole lot better than the value you get for second wines, that to me is the interesting thing to test.

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked: Great idea and line up Mark - will look forward to the results. I would echo '00 or '01 as good options, though '99 and '95 might be great candidates as well. The '96s I’ve had recently are still closed.

posts.name: Eric Egan
posts.cooked:
Fantastic idea for a blind tasting!

I’d throw my vote in for '90 and '04 as the best candidates, though '00 and '96 would also make an great tasting IMO. I think it might actually also be really interesting to go for a slightly lesser vintage like '93, but then I guess the merits of the vintage might end up being discussed as much as the intended topic…

posts.name: James Billy
posts.cooked:
Sorry for the thread drift, but could you describe the similarities between these 2 pairs of wine. I’m in no doubt that you know what you are talking about!

Mouton and Lynch Bages
Lafite and Ducru

posts.name: Marc Frontario
posts.cooked: just make sure smith haut lafitte is in there!

posts.name: Blake Brown
posts.cooked: Creative theme and one Id like to do here in Santa Barbara. My vote goes for the 90 vintage.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I would say that the vintage you chose will to some extent determine the answer you get.

Can’t think of any vintage, however, when Caruades de Lafite vs Ducru-Beaucaillou wouldn’t be a total KO. To find something on the level of Caruades, you will need to look for a Château like Rauzan-Gassies or Lynch Moussas.

Forts vs. GPL would be fun though, interested to hear the results.

fancy_title: First Growth second wines against first wines lesser growths.
title: First Growth second wines against first wines lesser growths.
